revision.text:
[[ar:دايجيرين (معجم)]][[en:Daijirin]][[fr:Daijirin]][[ja:大辞林]][[ru:Дайдзирин]][[uk:Дайдзірін]][[zh:大辞林]] [[File:『大辞林』第三版.jpg|thumb|200px|''Daijirin'' Third Edition]] {{italic title|qq=}} {{More footnotes|date=August 2015|qq=}} {{nihongo||大辞林|'''''Daijirin'''''|{{lit|Great Forest of Words|qq=}}|lead=yes|qq=}} is a comprehensive single-volume {{ill|Japanese dictionary|en|Japanese dictionary|qid=Q6158568|lt=Japanese dictionary|1=Japanese dictionary}} edited by {{nihongo|Akira Matsumura|松村明||''Matsumura Akira'', 1916–2001|qq=}}, and first published by {{nihongo|Sanseido Books|三省堂書店||''Sanseidō Shoten''|qq=}} in 1988. This title is based upon two early {{ill|Sanseidō|en|Sanseidō|qid=Q1058813|lt=Sanseidō|1=Sanseidō}} dictionaries edited by Shōzaburō Kanazawa (金沢庄三郎, 1872–1967), ''Jirin'' (辞林 "Forest of words", 1907) and the revised ''Kōjirin'' (広辞林 "Wide forest of words", 1925). ==History== [[File:Kanda-Jimbocho 2021.jpg|thumb|200px|A {{ill|Sanseidō|en|Sanseidō|qid=Q1058813|lt=Sanseidō|1=Sanseidō}} bookstore in [[:en:Jinbōchō, Tokyo|Jinbōchō, Tokyo]] (2021). An advertisement for ''Daijirin'' can be seen]] Sanseido specifically created ''Daijirin'' to compete with Iwanami's profitable ''{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}'' dictionary, which was a longtime bestseller through three editions (1955, 1969, and 1983). Two other contemporary dictionaries directed at the ''Kōjien'' market share were Kōdansha's color-illustrated ''{{ill|Nihongo Daijiten|en|Nihongo Daijiten|qid=Q7034322|lt=Nihongo Daijiten|1=Nihongo Daijiten}}'' (日本語大辞典 "Great dictionary of Japanese", 1989) and Shōgakukan's ''{{ill|Daijisen|en|Daijisen|qid=Q5209153|lt=Daijisen|1=Daijisen}}'' (大辞泉 "Great fountainhead of words", 1995, also edited by Akira Matsumura). The first edition of ''Daijirin'' (1988) had 220,000 {{ill|Headword|en|Headword|qid=Q3555190|lt=Headword|1=Headword}} entries and included encyclopedic content in numerous charts, tables, and illustrations. While ''Kōjien'' was printed in black and white, Sanseido included 19 two-color illustrations for topics like the seasons (with ''{{ill|Kigo|en|Kigo|qid=Q1085881|lt=Kigo|1=Kigo}}''), linguistics ({{ill|synonymy|en|synonymy|qid=Q31121203|lt=synonymy|1=Synonymy}}), and Japanese language (''{{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|qid=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}''). According to Matsumura's preface, the process of editing the first edition took over 28 years. The second edition (1995) increased the number of entries to 233,000 and augmented the number of illustrations (including 31 pages of full-color maps and charts). Sanseido published the second edition in printed, {{ill|CD-ROM|en|CD-ROM|qid=Q7982|lt=CD-ROM|1=CD-ROM}}, [[:en:E-book|E-book]], and Web versions. They additionally bundled the so-called "Super" version ''Sūpā Daijirin'' (スーパー大辞林) CD-ROM with other Sanseido Japanese and English dictionaries, plus pronunciation sound files. In 1997, Sanseido published a reverse dictionary of the second edition, entitled ''Kanji-biki, Gyaku-biki Daijirin'' (漢字引き・逆引き大辞林, {{ISBN|4-385-13901-6|qq=}}), with two indexes. The first lists ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' by ''[[:en:On-yomi|On-yomi]]'' and {{ill|stroke order|en|stroke order|qid=Q2991980|lt=stroke|1=Stroke order}} count, the second indexes headwords both by first and last ''kanji'' (for example, it lists ''jisho'' 辞書 "wordbook; dictionary" under both ''ji'' 辞 "word" and ''sho'' 書 "book"). According to Sanseido, total sales of the first two editions totaled over one million copies in 2003. The third edition (2006) added new headwords, such as the English {{ill|en:Loanword|en|en:Loanword|qid=Q103808|lt=en:Loanword|1=Loanword}} ''intarakutibu'' (インタラクティブ "interactive"), for a total of 238,000 entries. Japanese dictionary publishers have an ongoing dilemma, the increasing popularity of Internet and electronic dictionaries is decreasing the sales of printed ones. Kono (2007) notes, "According to Jiten Kyokai, an association of dictionary publishers, total annual sales of printed dictionaries, including popular Japanese and English, and specialized ones, such as technical dictionaries, halved to 6.5 million copies in the past decade." To promote the third edition, Sanseido launched a novel "Dual" service, the ''Dyuaru Daijirin'' (デュアル大辞林), allowing purchasers of the printed version to register for free online dictionary access. The online version is being regularly updated (currently{{when|date=April 2016|qq=}} including over 248,000 entries) and allows keyword searching for synonyms and related expressions. In 2006, collective ''Daijirin'' sales exceeded 1.5 million copies. ''Daijirin'' is also available on the Internet. Sanseido's Web Dictionary offers Web and {{ill|mobile phone|en|mobile phone|qid=Q17517|lt=mobile phone|1=Mobile phone}} subscription access to numerous dictionaries, including ''E-jirin'' (e辞林).{{cite web |title=三省堂 Web Dictionary サービス終了のお知らせ |url=https://www.sanseido-publ.co.jp/sp/wbdct_close.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201003194006/https://www.sanseido-publ.co.jp/sp/wbdct_close.html |archive-date=2020-10-03 |url-status=dead |language=ja|qq=}} {{ill|Nippon Telegraph and Telephone|en|Nippon Telegraph and Telephone|qid=Q1054787|lt=Nippon Telegraph and Telephone|1=Nippon Telegraph and Telephone}}'s "Goo Lab" provides a server that allows free online searching of the second edition ''Sūpā Daijirin''.[http://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/ goo 辞書] {{in lang|ja|qq=}} Yahoo also used to provide access to the second edition of the dictionary, but the service has since been discontinued.[http://dic.yahoo.co.jp/ Yahoo! Japanese Dictionary] {{in lang|ja|qq=}} ==Editions== ===Japanese dictionaries=== *Daijirin **1st edition ({{ISBN|4-385-14001-4|qq=}}) (1988-11-03) **2nd edition ({{ISBN|4-385-13900-8|qq=}}) (1995-11-03) **3rd edition ({{ISBN|4-385-13905-9|qq=}}) (大辞林 第三版) (2006-10-27): Includes 238,000 entries. **4th edition ({{ISBN|4-385-13906-7|qq=}}) (2019-09-05) *Dual Daijirin [Web edition] (Dual 大辞林［Web版］): It is a web browser-based version of Daijirin 3rd edition. ===Japanese-Chinese dictionaries=== *Shuangjierihancilin (雙解日漢辭林) ({{ISBN|978-957-11-4904-2|qq=}}, 1A88): Published by Wu-Nan Culture Enterprise(Wu-Nan Book Inc.), based on the Sanseido's Jirin 21 which is based on first edition of Daijirin. Includes 150,000 entries. **1st impression (2007-11-01) *Xinrihancilin (新日漢辭林) ({{ISBN|978-957-11-6084-9|qq=}}, 1A89): Published by Wu-Nan Culture Enterprise(Wu-Nan Book Inc.), based on the Daijirin 2nd edition. Includes 170,000 entries. **1st impression (2010-10-01) == Comparison with ''Kōjien'' == One of the biggest differences between ''Daijirin'' and ''Kōjien'' definitions is how they arrange meanings. A dictionary can arrange entries either historically with the oldest recorded meanings first (e.g., ''Kōjien'' and ''{{ill|Oxford English Dictionary|en|Oxford English Dictionary|qid=Q44996|lt=Oxford English Dictionary|1=Oxford English Dictionary}}'') or popularly with the most common meanings first (e.g., ''Daijirin'' and ''[[:en:American Heritage Dictionary|American Heritage Dictionary]]''). ''Daijirin'' entries encompass diverse vocabulary, including modern and {{ill|Classical Japanese|en|Classical Japanese|qid=Q1332057|lt=Classical Japanese|1=Classical Japanese}} words, scientific terminology, proper names, alphabetical abbreviations (like ''NG'' "no good; outtake, blooper"), and ''{{ill|Yojijukugo|en|Yojijukugo|qid=Q7010524|lt=Yojijukugo|1=Yojijukugo}}'' idioms. Some definitions include semantic notes distinguishing homonyms and synonyms. ''Daijirin'' usage examples range from classical texts like {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}} to modern publications. Tom Gally lists three advantages of ''Daijirin'',
Though ''Koujien'' is regarded by many in Japan as the authoritative dictionary and is the one most often cited by newspaper editorialists trying to make etymological points of questionable validity, I regard the best single-volume ''kokugo'' [Japanese language] dictionary to be ''Daijirin''. Designed to compete directly with ''Koujien'', ''Daijirin'' is different in one key way from its illustrious predecessor: whereas ''Koujien'' arranges the senses of its definitions in historical order, ''Daijirin'' puts the most common contemporary meanings first. The result, for a person reading modern Japanese, is that ''Daijirin'' is the most likely to list the intended meaning where it can be found easily.{{sfn|Gally|1999|p=|qq=}}
The other two ''Daijirin'' advantages are semantically "more detailed" definitions and the "unusual, though not unprecedented" ''kanji'' and reverse-dictionary index. Baroni and Bialock describe ''Daijirin'',
This is the most up-to-date and attractive of the large single-volume ''kokugo jiten''. In this sense, it may overlap or even supersede ''Kōjien'' in neologism and ''[[:en:Gairaigo|Gairaigo]]''. It also features illustrations and historical references, charts, and explanations of historic or complicated terms. It is visually easier to use, with ''[[:en:gojuon|gojuon]]'' headings clearly boxed off, and uses larger headings for more significant entries.{{sfn|Baroni|Bialock|2005|p=|qq=}}
Faris writes,
In general the definitions in the ''Daijirin'' are fairly easy to read, while in many cases a non-native of Japanese would have more trouble reading ''Kōjien'' definitions, which often contain words more difficult than the one they are defining. There are also many cases where the ''Daijirin'' is simply more complete, and contains usage or definitions not given in the ''Kōjien''.{{sfn|Faris|2005|p=|qq=}}
He compares the definitions for ''abarenbō'' (暴れん坊), literally meaning "rambunctious kid, wild child; bundle of energy" and figuratively meaning ''abaremono'' (暴れ者) "rowdy; hooligan; tough; maverick". *''Kōjien'': 思うままに振舞う人。あばれもの。"A person who behaves however he wants. A violent person." *''Daijirin'': 1. けんかやいたずらをする活発な子供。2. 周囲を気にせず強引な行動をする人。"1. An energetic child that gets into fights and causes mischief. 2. A person who behaves forcefully without regard for people around him." Faris concludes that since, "The most popular use of this word is with regard to overactive children, so the ''Daijirin'' wins in this case." ==References== {{reflist|25em|qq=}} ===Sources=== *{{cite web |last1=Baroni |first1=Helen |first2=David |last2=Bialock |year=2005 |url=http://www.columbia.edu/~hds2/BIB95/ch11.htm |title=Words: Japanese Dictionaries |website=Columbia University |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200130083130/https://www.columbia.edu/~hds2/BIB95/ch11.htm |archive-date=2020-01-30 |url-status=dead|qq=}} *{{cite web |last=Faris |first=Quinlan |date=2005-03-26 |url=http://quinlanfaris.com/?p=17 |title=Japanese Dictionaries – The Kōjien vs. the Super Daijirin |series=Tokyo Tsure Zure Gusa |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120509004549/http://quinlanfaris.com/?p=17 |archive-date=2012-05-09 |url-status=dead|qq=}} *{{cite web |last=Gally |first=Tom |year=1999 |url=http://www.gally.net/translation/kokugo.htm |title=Kokugo Dictionaries 国語辞書 |access-date=2020-10-25|qq=}}, review article ==External links== *Sanseido pages: [http://daijirin.dual-d.net/ Daijirin 3rd edition], [http://djr1tri.dual-d.net/ Dual Daijirin] {{in lang|ja|qq=}} *Wu-Nan Book Inc pages: [https://web.archive.org/web/20071230080422/http://www.wunan.com.tw/bookdetail.asp?no=8915 Shuangjierihancilin], [http://www.wunan.com.tw/bookdetail.asp?no=10179 Xinrihancilin] {{Dictionaries of Japanese|qq=}} [[en:Daijirin]] [[fr:Daijirin]] [[ja:大辞林]] [[ru:Дайдзирин]] [[uk:Дайдзірін]] [[zh:大辞林]] {{wikidata link|Q5209149}} [[Category:1988 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1988 non-fiction books]] [[Category:Japanese dictionaries|Daijirin]] [[Category:Lexicography]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:1988 books]] [[Category:IOS software]] [[Category:三省堂の国語辞典]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 100+ untranslated japanese characters]]
